title: The Handshake and the Warplanes
subtitle: Xi met Taiwan's opposition leader in Beijing while sending 16 warplanes toward the island. The signal was not diplomatic.

sources.angle: Straight news: Taiwan defense ministry confirmed 16 PLA aircraft near the island during the Beijing meeting; frames the dual signal as notable but avoids calling it deliberate.
Focuses on Xi's peace framing and his contrast with ongoing global conflicts; reads the meeting as Xi positioning China as a stabilizing force before the Trump summit.
Contextualizes the meeting within the upcoming Trump-Xi summit; highlights that the KMT's presence in Beijing gives Xi domestic-politics cover for a softer cross-strait posture.
Quotes Xi directly: reunification is 'inevitable' and 'people on both sides of the strait are Chinese'; emphasizes Xi's confidence framing over any military subtext.
Focuses on the abnormally high number of PLA Navy ships at sea simultaneously, tying it explicitly to US military attention being diverted toward Iran.

brief.whatHappened: On April 10, Chinese President Xi Jinping met Cheng Li-wun, chairwoman of Taiwan's Kuomintang opposition party, in Beijing. It was the first such meeting between Xi and a senior Taiwanese political figure in nearly a decade. While the meeting was underway, Taiwan's defense ministry detected 16 Chinese military aircraft and 7 naval vessels operating near the island. Xi declared reunification 'inevitable' and framed cross-strait ties as a shared Chinese heritage. The meeting came days before a scheduled summit between Xi and Trump.
brief.bottomLine: Xi used the same 24-hour window to run a diplomatic charm offensive on Taiwan's opposition and a military pressure demonstration against its government. Both messages were intentional, and both were aimed at the same audience: Washington.

hiddenBets.assumption: The KMT meeting signals China seeking dialogue over confrontation
hiddenBets.whyItMightBeWrong: China has run this play before: invite the opposition, declare cooperation inevitable, then use the visit to delegitimize the elected government. The warplanes flying concurrently suggest the meeting was cover for pressure, not a substitute for it.

hiddenBets.assumption: The US is the decisive external variable in cross-strait stability
hiddenBets.whyItMightBeWrong: Taiwan's internal politics may matter more. If the KMT wins the next cycle, China gets the interlocutor it wants without any invasion. Xi may be investing in electoral politics rather than military options.

hiddenBets.assumption: Timing the warplanes with the diplomatic meeting was accidental or routine
hiddenBets.whyItMightBeWrong: An unusually high number of PLA Navy ships were also at sea simultaneously, correlated with Washington's attention being focused on Iran. Routine operations are not typically timed to opposition-leader summits.

brief.realDisagreement: The fork: Is China running a dual-track strategy where diplomacy and military pressure are complementary tools, or is the KMT meeting a genuine attempt to find an off-ramp that Beijing would honor if the right party won in Taipei? The first reading means the pressure will persist regardless. The second means the cross-strait conflict is more negotiable than the last decade suggests. The warplane timing leans toward the first. But Xi's explicit confidence that reunification will happen without war, combined with the effort to cultivate opposition relationships, suggests he believes he can win the political competition before the military one becomes necessary. The honest answer is he is doing both simultaneously and will use whichever proves effective.
brief.whatNoOneIsSaying: Taiwan's ruling Democratic Progressive Party cannot say out loud that the KMT meeting in Beijing may have been more consequential than any military exercise this year. Admitting that domestic electoral politics represent a greater near-term risk to Taiwan's status than PLA aircraft would hand the opposition a policy argument it does not deserve to win on those terms.

whoPays.how: Every KMT-Beijing meeting frames the ruling party as the obstacle to cross-strait peace rather than the defender of sovereignty. The framing compounds domestically ahead of elections.
whoPays.when: Accumulates over the next 12-18 months before the next electoral cycle

whoPays.who: US defense planners in the Pacific
whoPays.how: Each demonstration of PLA readiness during a diplomatic opening reduces the interpretive clarity of Chinese military signals. When everything is ambiguous, deterrence gets harder to calibrate.
Political uncertainty depresses cross-strait investment and creates risk premiums even when no military event occurs. The volatility is the cost.

scenarios.name: Electoral Wedge
scenarios.whatHappens: The KMT uses the Beijing meeting to campaign on cross-strait stability. If they win Taiwan's next election, China gets the political outcome it wants without firing a shot.
scenarios.signal: KMT poll numbers rise in the months following the Beijing visit; Beijing makes further gestures toward economic normalization with Taiwan.

scenarios.name: Summit Leverage
scenarios.whatHappens: Xi uses the KMT meeting as a signal to Trump that China has Taiwan covered diplomatically, extracting concessions on trade or technology at the summit.
scenarios.signal: Xi and Trump announce a 'stability framework' for cross-strait relations at their upcoming summit that implicitly sidelines Taipei.

scenarios.name: Pressure Escalation
scenarios.whatHappens: The diplomatic meeting produces no results Beijing values. PLA exercises around Taiwan intensify through summer as China signals impatience.
scenarios.signal: PLA exercises move closer to Taiwan's territorial sea baseline or include live-fire components within 50km of the island.

brief.whatWouldChange: If Taiwan's defense ministry confirmed the warplane sortie was pre-planned and unrelated to the Beijing meeting, the dual-track reading weakens considerably. Or if the KMT publicly rejects any constitutional framework that does not preserve Taiwan's separate governance, the 'electoral wedge' scenario collapses.

markets.question: Will China invade Taiwan by end of 2026?
China x Taiwan military clash before 2027?
